title: Teams and Roles
description: Configure context teams, shared teams, and team templates. Understand role types, team instances, and how role resolution drives access control and workflow assignment.

topics.title: Team Management Fundamentals

content.text: Context managers such as product and project managers use simplified team management to indirectly manage access control and workflow assignment. Rather than configuring security policies directly, they assign participants to roles on a team page, and Windchill resolves those assignments into access control rules and workflow task routing.
The Context Manager's Role

Context managers work with teams by adding participants to predefined roles. Each role carries meaning in terms of what data a participant can access and which workflow tasks they receive. The manager does not need to understand the underlying access control policies or workflow routing logic - assigning the right people to the right roles is sufficient.

Context managers do not need full administrative knowledge of Windchill security. The team page abstracts the complexity of access control and workflow configuration into a simple participant-to-role assignment model.
How Teams Drive Access and Workflows

rows: Participants in roles determine what data they can access
Generic roles map to specific users who receive workflow tasks
Teams map generic business roles to specific participants

content.prompt: Match each team management concept to its description.

keyTakeaways: Context managers assign participants to roles, indirectly controlling access and workflow routing.
Generic roles define which users receive workflow tasks and what data they can access.
Teams bridge the gap between business role definitions and Windchill security enforcement.

topics.title: Context Team Architecture and Roles

content.text: Every product, project, library, and program in Windchill has exactly one context team. This team manages context roles visible on the Members table and context groups generated in the database. Understanding how roles, groups, and the special Team Members group interact is essential for configuring access control correctly.
Context Roles and Context Groups

The Team Members group is a superset of all non-Guest participants. Editing out-of-the-box access control rules on this group affects every team member. Always review rules carefully before modifying this group.
Resource Bundle Roles vs Context Roles

content.prompt: Arrange the steps in the correct order for what happens when a user is added to a context role.
items: User is added to the context role
System generates a corresponding context group entry
User is added to the Team Members group
Access control rules for the context group take effect

keyTakeaways: Every context has one context team with roles visible on the Members table and corresponding groups in Policy Administration.
The Team Members group collects all non-Guest participants and has no corresponding role on the team page.
Resource bundle roles can be used in workflows and lifecycles; context team roles are limited to access control resolution.
Adding a user to a context role automatically generates a context group entry and adds the user to the Team Members group.

topics.title: Shared Teams

content.text: Shared teams are defined at the organization level and can be used across multiple products, libraries, projects, and programs. They provide a centralized way to manage team membership when the same participants and roles apply to many contexts.
How Shared Teams Work

cards.back: Defined at the organization level and shared across multiple products, libraries, projects, or programs.
When enabled, context managers can add roles and members locally but cannot remove shared team entries.
Updates to a shared team are reflected in all associated contexts. Use Show Team Uses to see affected contexts.

content.text: Always use the Show Team Uses query before making changes to a shared team. This report lists every context that uses the shared team, helping you understand the full impact of your updates.

content.prompt: Match each team type to its defining characteristic.

pairs.left: Defined at product or project level
Can be used across multiple contexts

keyTakeaways: Shared teams are defined at the organization level and reused across multiple contexts.
Context managers can extend shared teams locally when the option is enabled but cannot remove shared entries.
Changes to a shared team automatically propagate to all associated contexts.

topics.title: Team Templates and Team Instances

content.text: Team templates define the roles needed for advanced business processes such as promotion requests and change workflows. When an object initiates a process, Windchill generates a team instance by resolving roles and participants from multiple sources in a specific order.
Team Template Definition

Team templates can be created at the site, organization, or context level. When defining a template, you specify its name, description, and enabled status, then assign roles drawn from the resource bundle. These roles define the positions that must be filled when a process starts.
Team Instance Resolution

content.prompt: Arrange the team instance resolution steps in the correct order.
items: Team Template adds roles to the team instance
Life Cycle Template adds any missing roles
Context Team populates participants into existing roles
Workflow adds any missing process roles

content.text: Once a team instance is created, it is unaffected by subsequent changes to the team template or context team. Only team instances created after a change will reflect the updated configuration.
Best Practices

rows: Use context team as master for role resolution
Simplifies administration - one place to manage participants
Don't add all roles to templates as a safety net
Creates confusion with long lists of unused and unresolved roles
Only resolve org-level roles in team templates
Product and project managers should not manage organization-level positions
Same role name must resolve to the same person across all workflows

keyTakeaways: Team templates define roles for business processes and can be created at site, organization, or context level.
Team instance resolution follows a specific order: team template, lifecycle template, context team, then workflow.
Once created, team instances are unaffected by changes to their source templates or context teams.
Use the context team as the master source for participant resolution to simplify administration.

topics.title: Exercise - Administer Roles and Context Teams

content.text: In this exercise you will add a custom role to an organization and then configure a product context team by adding roles, removing unnecessary roles, and assigning participants.
Sign in as bpatterson/ptc to complete this exercise.

content.title: Administer Roles and Context Teams
content.objective: Add a custom Editor role to the PTC Publications organization and configure the product context team with appropriate roles and members.

tasks.title: Add Roles to the PTC Publications Organization Context

steps.action: Launch a web browser and select the Windchill bookmark.
steps.detail: Open a browser to access the Windchill server.

steps.action: Select Sign In.
steps.detail: The Windchill sign-in page appears.

steps.action: Sign in to Windchill as a business administrator.
steps.detail: Use the business administrator credentials to access administrative functions.

steps.action: Browse to the PTC Publications organization and select the Roles link.
steps.detail: Navigate to the Roles management page for the PTC Publications organization.

steps.action: Click the New roles to organization icon.
steps.detail: This opens the Add Roles to Organization dialog where you can create new context roles.

steps.action: In the Add roles to organization dialog, type Editor.
steps.detail: This creates a new custom role named Editor for the PTC Publications organization.

steps.action: Click OK. Verify the new role is listed in the Roles table.
steps.detail: Upon creation, the roles have Visible status to make them selectable in the contexts located inside the organization: products, libraries, projects, and programs.

steps.action: In the Navigator, select Recent Products.
steps.detail: You should still be signed in to Windchill as bpatterson/ptc.

steps.action: Expand the PTC New Publication Template product and click the Team link.
steps.detail: This opens the context team page for the product, showing current roles and members.

steps.action: Select Actions > Add Roles.
steps.detail: This opens the Add Roles dialog where you can add organization-level or resource bundle roles to the context team.

steps.action: Select Editor from the role list and click OK.
steps.detail: The Editor role you created in the previous task is now added to the product context team.

steps.action: In the Members table, select the Roles/Members column header to sort by ascending order.
steps.detail: Sorting the table makes it easier to find and select the roles you want to remove.

steps.action: Select the checkboxes to remove: Change Admin I, Change Admin II, Change Admin III, Change Review Board, Option Manager, Package Creator, and Variance Approver.
steps.detail: These roles are not needed for the PTC Publications context and should be removed to keep the template clean.

steps.action: Click the Remove roles or participants icon.
steps.detail: The selected roles are removed from the context team.
steps.hint: It is a best practice to remove all unnecessary roles in a product context that will generate a product template. Any new contexts created with this template will have, by default, only the roles defined in the template.

steps.action: In the Editor row, click the Add participants to team icon.
steps.detail: This opens a search dialog where you can find and assign participants to the Editor role.

steps.action: Search for David Griffin (dgriffin: PTC Publications).
steps.detail: Find the David Griffin user account in the PTC Publications organization.

steps.action: Add the user to the Participant List and click OK.
steps.detail: David Griffin is now assigned to the Editor role in the product context team.

steps.action: From the View menu, select Members.
steps.detail: This switches the table view to show all named members and their role assignments.

steps.action: Notice the two named user accounts and one group with assigned roles.
steps.detail: The Members view confirms that the context team now includes the correct participants.

tasks.title: Add Existing and Custom Roles to Context Team

steps.action: From the View menu, select Local Team Roles and Members.
steps.detail: This view shows only the roles and members defined locally for this product context team.

steps.action: Select Actions > Add Roles.
steps.detail: This opens the Add Roles dialog to add additional roles to the team.

steps.action: In the Add Roles to Team dialog, press Ctrl and select Change Manager, Consultant, and Design Team Leader from the list.
steps.detail: Hold Ctrl to select multiple resource bundle roles at once.

steps.action: In the text field, type Managing Editor,Visual Designer.
steps.detail: These two custom roles are typed directly because they are ad hoc roles that don't exist in the resource bundle yet.
steps.hint: Custom roles created ad hoc for a specific team are not available for use in workflows. Only roles created using the Enumerated Customized Type Utility can be used in workflows.

steps.action: Click OK to add the new roles to the product Context Team.
steps.detail: The context team now includes both the selected resource bundle roles and the custom typed roles.

keyTakeaways: Custom roles are added at the organization level and then become available to context teams.
Context teams should only include roles relevant to the product or project to reduce clutter.
Removing unnecessary roles from product templates is a best practice for streamlined team management.

topics.title: Exercise - Use Shared Teams

content.text: In this exercise you will create a shared team at the organization level, enable local extensions, and then create a new product that uses the shared team.
Complete the previous exercise before starting this one. You must be signed in as bpatterson/ptc.

content.title: Use Shared Teams
content.objective: Create a shared team for PTC Publications and test it by creating a new product that uses the shared team.

steps.action: From the Navigator, browse to Organizations > PTC Publications > Teams.
steps.detail: You should still be signed in to Windchill as bpatterson/ptc. Navigate to the Teams page at the organization level.

steps.action: From the Shared Teams toolbar, select the New shared team icon.
steps.detail: This opens the New Shared Team dialog where you define the team name and settings.

steps.action: In the Team Name field, type PTC Publication Manuals.
steps.detail: This names the shared team that will be used across multiple PTC Publications products.

steps.action: Select the checkbox for Allow team to be extended locally in associated application contexts and click OK.
steps.detail: This enables product or project managers to add roles and members locally while preserving the shared team's core membership.

steps.action: From the Shared Teams list, select the View information icon for the PTC Publication Manuals shared team.
steps.detail: This opens the shared team details page where you can manage roles and members.

steps.action: In the Members table, add David Griffin to the Members role.
steps.detail: David Griffin is now a member of the shared team and will be included in any context that uses this team.

tasks.title: Create a Product Using a Shared Team

steps.action: From the Navigator, click Recent Products and select View All.
steps.detail: This opens the full product list where you can create new products.

steps.action: From the Products toolbar, click New Product.
steps.detail: This opens the New Product dialog where you configure the product name, template, and team.

steps.action: In the New Product dialog, click Find. Then, in the Find Shared Team dialog, click Search.
steps.detail: This searches for available shared teams that can be associated with the new product.

steps.action: Select PTC Publication Manuals and click OK.
steps.detail: The shared team is now associated with the new product.

steps.action: Click OK on the warning message related to the product Private access option deactivation.
steps.detail: Using a shared team requires deactivating the Private access option so that shared team members can access the product.

steps.action: Select the checkbox for Allow roles and/or members to be added locally in this context.
steps.detail: This enables the product manager to extend the shared team with local roles and members specific to this product.

steps.action: In the Name field, type Maintenance Manuals.
steps.detail: This sets the product name for the new product that will use the shared team.

steps.action: From the Template menu, select General Product and click OK.
steps.detail: The General Product template provides a standard set of context roles and access control rules for the new product.

steps.action: From the Navigator, browse to PTC Publications > Teams.
steps.detail: Navigate back to the organization-level Teams page to verify the shared team association.

steps.action: From the Shared Team list, right-click PTC Publication Manuals and select Show Team Uses.
steps.detail: This runs a Where Used query to display all contexts that use this shared team.

steps.action: Notice that the Maintenance Manuals product is listed in the Shared Team Uses Report.
steps.detail: This confirms the shared team was successfully associated with the new product.

steps.action: Close the browser.
steps.detail: The exercise is complete.

keyTakeaways: Shared teams are created at the organization level and can be associated with multiple products.
The Allow team to be extended locally option lets context managers add roles and members without modifying the shared team.
Show Team Uses provides a report of all contexts associated with a shared team.

topics.title: Exercise - Create Team Templates

content.text: In this exercise you will create a team template for the Agenda document type, assign roles from the resource bundle, and configure an object initialization rule to apply the template automatically.
Complete the previous exercises before starting this one. You must be signed in as bpatterson/ptc.

content.title: Create Team Templates
content.objective: Create a team template for the Agenda document type and assign it using object initialization rules.

steps.action: From the Navigator, browse to Recent Products > PTC New Publication Template > Utilities.
steps.detail: You should still be signed in to Windchill as bpatterson/ptc. Navigate to the product utilities page.

steps.action: Select Team Administration.
steps.detail: This opens the Team Administration page where you can create and manage team templates.

steps.action: Click the New Team template icon and expand the dialog.
steps.detail: This opens the New Team Template dialog where you define the template name and roles.

steps.action: Type Agenda Team in the Name field.
steps.detail: This names the team template that will be assigned to the Agenda document type.

steps.action: Click the Add Role icon.
steps.detail: This opens the Find Participants dialog where you can search for and add roles to the template.

steps.action: In the Find Participants dialog, type Manag* in the search field, and click Search.
steps.detail: This searches for roles matching the wildcard pattern to find Managing Editor.

steps.action: Select the Managing Editor checkbox and click Apply.
steps.detail: The Managing Editor role is added to the team template. Click Apply to keep the dialog open for the next role.

steps.action: Type proj* in the search field, then click Search.
steps.detail: This searches for the Project Engineer role.

steps.action: Select the Project Engineer checkbox and click OK.
steps.detail: The Project Engineer role is added to the team template and the dialog closes.

steps.action: Confirm the two roles/participants are listed in the table.
steps.detail: Both Managing Editor and Project Engineer should appear in the roles list.
steps.hint: As a best practice, do not add participants in the team template. Assign users or groups to roles within the context team.

steps.action: Click OK to close the New Team Template dialog.
steps.detail: The Agenda Team template is now created and available for assignment.

tasks.title: Assign Team Template to the Agenda Document Type

steps.action: Browse to the PTC New Publication Template product and select the Utilities link.
steps.detail: Navigate back to the product utilities page to access Object Initialization Rules.
steps.hint: If you want to automatically apply the same object initialization rule for all products and libraries, you can store the Agenda Object initialization rule and the Agenda Team template in the organization context.

steps.action: Select the Object Initialization Rules Administration link in the Business Administration section.
steps.detail: This opens the Object Initialization Rules table where you can create rules that automatically assign templates to object types.

steps.action: Click the New Object Initialization Rule icon.
steps.detail: This opens the New Object Initialization Rule dialog.

steps.action: Type Agenda Rule in the Name field.
steps.detail: This names the initialization rule that will link the Agenda document type to the team template.

steps.action: In the Type field, select Find.
steps.detail: This opens the Object Type browser where you can select the document type.

steps.action: In the Object Type dialog, expand the Document node, select the Agenda radio button, and click OK.
steps.detail: This sets the rule to apply specifically to Agenda-type documents.

steps.action: Click Choose File and click This PC. Browse to W:\WCBA-TEAM-Lab-Files\Agenda_Default_TeamOIR.xml and click Open to load the initialization rule.
steps.detail: This XML file defines the lifecycle and team template to be used for any new Agenda document type.
steps.hint: The Agenda_Default_Team.xml defines the life cycle and team template to be used for any new agenda document type. The team template is only used with an advanced life cycle.

steps.action: Click OK to complete the Agenda Rule initialization rule definition.
steps.detail: The initialization rule is now active and will automatically assign the Agenda Team template to new Agenda documents.

steps.action: From the Navigator, browse to the PTC New Publication Template product and click the Folders link.
steps.detail: Navigate to the product's folder structure to create a test document.

steps.action: Click the New Document icon.
steps.detail: This opens the New Document dialog where you can select the document type and enter details.

steps.action: Select the Agenda type, set the Primary Content Source to No Content, and type Agenda Team Test in the Name field.
steps.detail: Creating an Agenda-type document will trigger the initialization rule you just configured.

steps.action: Click Finish.
steps.detail: The Agenda document is created with the initialization rule applied.

steps.action: View the Information page for the Agenda Team Test.
steps.detail: The created document has a state of In Work, the first state of the created lifecycle. Notice that the Team Template is Agenda Team and the Life Cycle Template is set to Default.

steps.action: Close the web browser session.
steps.detail: The exercise is complete. The Agenda Team template is properly assigned to the Agenda document type through the initialization rule.

keyTakeaways: Team templates define the roles needed for specific business processes or document types.
Object initialization rules automatically assign team templates when new objects are created.
Roles added to team templates come from the resource bundle and are used for workflow and lifecycle resolution.
